title: Original Longer MBE-Style Priority Practice Questions - Batch 008
description: Thirty-five original, AI-assisted, longer-form legacy MBE-style question drafts. These are not NCBE questions and are not endorsed by NCBE. Drafted with longer fact patterns to more closely mirror the density and pacing of public MBE sample questions.

rights.marketingRestriction: Do not call these official, released, licensed, actual, real, or NCBE questions. Do not imply NCBE affiliation or endorsement.

styleNotes: Longer stems are intentional and are designed to approximate the factual density of public MBE-style sample questions.
Each item uses a realistic factual scenario, a distinct call of the question, and four parallel options.
Each item tests one principal rule, occasionally with one closely related rule.
Each distractor reflects a plausible legal error and receives an independent explanation.
No official NCBE question text is copied or lightly rewritten.

materials: User-supplied NCBE MBE sample questions, consulted only for general length, pacing, four-option structure, and question formatting.
Current Federal Rules of Civil Procedure and Federal Rules of Evidence from U.S. Courts for federal-rule-dependent civil procedure and evidence items.
Generally accepted common-law, UCC Article 2, constitutional, criminal procedure, torts, property, and evidence doctrines identified in the authority notes.

structuredChecksPassed: Exactly four options and one keyed answer per question
Independent explanation, governing rule, application, trap, and memory aid
Longer-form stems targeted to public MBE-style factual density
Unique keys and no prior-batch key-prefix collision by using longform key namespace
All items remain blocked from publication pending qualified review
limitations: These questions are original drafts, not official NCBE questions.
The internal audit is not an independent legal opinion or psychometric validation.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Seventh Amendment - common factual issues in legal and equitable claims
questions.stem: A software company sued a former employee in federal court after the employee left to work for a competitor. The complaint sought damages for breach of a nondisclosure agreement and also sought an injunction requiring the employee to return copies of confidential source code. The employee denied copying any source code and timely demanded a jury trial. The company asked the judge to hold a short evidentiary hearing first and decide whether the employee had copied the code, arguing that the injunction request was equitable and that an early ruling would simplify the damages claim. The disputed copying issue is essential to both the damages claim and the injunction request. No party disputed federal subject-matter jurisdiction, and the employee did not consent to a bench trial. The proposed hearing would require live testimony from the same engineers who would testify to the jury about copying.
questions.call: How should the court handle the disputed copying issue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The jury should decide the factual issue for the legal damages claim before the judge resolves equitable relief.
choices.rationale: When legal and equitable claims share a factual issue, the jury right on the legal claim must be preserved by trying common facts to the jury first.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The judge should decide the issue first because the company filed an equitable request for an injunction.
choices.rationale: The presence of equitable relief does not allow the court to decide common factual issues first in a way that defeats the jury right on legal damages.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The judge should decide the issue because copying source code is too technical for a civil jury.
choices.rationale: Technical complexity alone does not eliminate the Seventh Amendment right to a jury on legal claims.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The jury should decide both whether code was copied and whether the injunction should issue.
choices.rationale: The jury decides disputed facts tied to legal claims; the judge decides the ultimate equitable remedy.

explanation.testedIssue: How a federal court should sequence common factual issues when a case combines legal damages and equitable injunctive relief.
explanation.governingRule: When legal and equitable claims share factual issues, the issues common to the legal claim generally must be tried to the jury before the court resolves equitable claims.
explanation.application: The employee timely demanded a jury, and the copying issue is central to the damages claim. The court may later decide whether equitable relief is appropriate, but it should not first make binding factual findings on copying.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that the equitable claim lets the judge decide overlapping facts before the jury hears the legal claim.
explanation.memoryAid: Common facts go to the jury first when legal damages are in the case.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Erie doctrine - forum state's choice-of-law rules
questions.stem: A citizen of State A and a citizen of State B were injured in a boating accident on a lake located in State C. The State A citizen sued the State B citizen in federal district court in State B, invoking diversity jurisdiction. State A law would allow punitive damages on the facts alleged, State C law would not, and State B uses a choice-of-law rule that applies the law of the state with the most significant relationship to the accident. The plaintiff argued that, because the case is in federal court, the judge should apply a general federal choice-of-law approach that favors the law of the plaintiff's domicile. The defendant did not identify any Federal Rule or federal statute that supplied a competing choice-of-law standard. Both parties agreed that the underlying negligence and damages claims were created by state law.
questions.call: Which choice-of-law approach should the federal court use?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A general federal common-law choice-of-law rule, because the case is pending in federal court.
choices.rationale: A diversity court ordinarily does not create its own choice-of-law rules for state-law claims.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The choice-of-law rules of State B, the forum state in which the federal court sits.
choices.rationale: A federal court sitting in diversity applies the choice-of-law rules of the state in which it sits.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The law of State A, because the plaintiff chose a federal forum to vindicate a state-law claim.
choices.rationale: The plaintiff's domicile and choice of federal court do not control the choice-of-law framework.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The law of State C automatically, because the accident occurred there and no further analysis is permitted.
choices.rationale: The accident location may matter under the forum state's rules, but the federal court must first use State B's choice-of-law rules.

explanation.testedIssue: Which choice-of-law rules a federal diversity court applies.
explanation.governingRule: A federal court sitting in diversity applies state substantive law, including the forum state's choice-of-law rules.
explanation.application: The federal court sits in State B. It therefore applies State B's choice-of-law methodology to decide whether State A, State B, or State C substantive law governs.
explanation.commonTrap: Jumping straight to the law with the most appealing substantive result instead of identifying the correct choice-of-law system.
explanation.memoryAid: Diversity court: use the forum state's conflicts rules.

authorityNotes: Klaxon Co. v. Stentor Electric Manufacturing Co.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Issue preclusion - default judgment and actual litigation
questions.stem: A pedestrian sued a delivery company in federal court after being struck by one of the company's vans. The complaint alleged that the van driver ran a red light. The delivery company was properly served but failed to answer, and the court entered a default judgment for the pedestrian. The judgment awarded damages but made no factual findings other than those necessary to support the default. Several months later, a passenger in the same van brought a separate negligence action against the delivery company and sought to prevent the company from disputing that the driver ran the red light, relying on the default judgment in the pedestrian's case. The passenger was not a party to the pedestrian's case and had no control over that litigation. The delivery company had not filed any admissions, stipulations, or discovery responses in the first action.
questions.call: Should the court give the default judgment issue-preclusive effect on the red-light issue?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because any final judgment on the merits conclusively establishes every allegation in the complaint in later litigation.
choices.rationale: Claim preclusion may follow a final default judgment, but issue preclusion generally requires actual litigation of the issue.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the delivery company had notice and chose not to appear in the first action.
choices.rationale: Notice and opportunity are important, but actual litigation is ordinarily required for issue preclusion.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the red-light issue was not actually litigated in the default action.
choices.rationale: A default judgment ordinarily does not have issue-preclusive effect because the issues were not actually contested and determined.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because issue preclusion can be used only by a party who was also a party to the first action.
choices.rationale: Nonmutual issue preclusion may sometimes be available; the problem here is the absence of actual litigation.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a default judgment establishes a factual issue for purposes of collateral estoppel in later litigation.
explanation.governingRule: Issue preclusion generally requires that the identical issue was actually litigated, actually determined, essential to a valid final judgment, and asserted against a party who had a full and fair opportunity to litigate.
explanation.application: The delivery company defaulted. The driver’s red-light conduct was alleged and admitted for default purposes, but it was not actually contested or decided after litigation.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating every effect of a default judgment as issue preclusion.
explanation.memoryAid: Default can end a claim, but it usually does not actually litigate an issue.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Effect of timely posttrial motion on civil notice-of-appeal period
questions.stem: After a federal jury trial in a diversity action, the district court entered final judgment for the defendant on March 1. On March 14, the plaintiff timely filed a motion for a new trial under Rule 59, arguing that an erroneous jury instruction likely affected the verdict. The district court denied the Rule 59 motion on April 10. On May 5, the plaintiff filed a notice of appeal from the final judgment and from the order denying a new trial. The defendant moved to dismiss the appeal as untimely, arguing that more than 30 days had passed since the March 1 judgment. No federal agency or United States party was involved. The Rule 59 motion was filed within the time permitted by the Civil Rules, and no separate extension of time to appeal was requested.
questions.call: Is the plaintiff's notice of appeal timely?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the 30-day appeal period always runs from entry of the original final judgment.
choices.rationale: Certain timely postjudgment motions suspend or reset the time to appeal until the motion is resolved.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because a Rule 59 motion extends the appeal period only if the district court grants the motion.
choices.rationale: A timely Rule 59 motion affects the appeal period regardless of whether it is granted or denied.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only because the case was based on diversity rather than federal-question jurisdiction.
choices.rationale: The timing effect of a timely Rule 59 motion does not turn on the source of subject-matter jurisdiction.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, because the timely Rule 59 motion postponed the appeal deadline until after the order disposing of that motion.
choices.rationale: A timely Rule 59 motion resets the time to appeal, and the notice filed within 30 days after denial is timely.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a timely Rule 59 motion affects the deadline for filing a civil notice of appeal.
explanation.governingRule: A timely Rule 59 motion is among the postjudgment motions that delay the running of the civil appeal period until entry of the order disposing of the motion.
explanation.application: The plaintiff timely moved for a new trial and filed the notice within 30 days after the court denied that motion.
explanation.commonTrap: Counting only from the original judgment and overlooking postjudgment motions that affect appellate timing.
explanation.memoryAid: Timely Rule 59 motion: appeal clock waits for the ruling.

questions.subject: Civil Procedure
questions.subtopic: Collateral-order review - denial of qualified immunity on a legal issue
questions.stem: A protester sued a city police officer in federal court under 42 U.S.C. § 1983, alleging that the officer arrested her for criticizing the mayor at a public meeting. The officer moved to dismiss on qualified-immunity grounds. The district court accepted the complaint's well-pleaded factual allegations as true and held that, if those allegations were proved, they would show a violation of clearly established First Amendment rights. The court denied the motion. The officer immediately filed a notice of appeal, limiting the appeal to the purely legal question whether the alleged facts described a violation of clearly established law. The officer does not ask the appellate court to decide whether the protester's version of events is true. The appeal assumes those facts and challenges only the legal sufficiency of the alleged constitutional violation.
questions.call: May the court of appeals hear the immediate appeal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because a denial of qualified immunity is immediately appealable to the extent it turns on a legal question.
choices.rationale: Qualified immunity protects against the burdens of litigation, and a denial presenting a legal issue can fall within the collateral-order doctrine.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only if the district court certifies the order for interlocutory appeal under 28 U.S.C. § 1292(b).
choices.rationale: Certification is not required for this category of collateral-order appeal when the qualified-immunity issue is legal.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because an order denying a motion to dismiss can never be appealed before final judgment.
choices.rationale: Most denials of motions to dismiss are not immediately appealable, but qualified-immunity denials on legal questions are an important exception.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because qualified immunity can be reviewed only after the officer has gone through trial.
choices.rationale: The point of qualified immunity includes avoiding trial when the legal standard is not met.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether denial of qualified immunity may be appealed before final judgment.
explanation.governingRule: Under the collateral-order doctrine, a denial of qualified immunity is immediately appealable to the extent the appeal presents a legal question rather than a dispute about what facts the evidence proves.
explanation.application: The officer accepts the pleaded facts for purposes of the appeal and challenges only whether those facts violate clearly established law.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that every interlocutory appeal requires certification or that no immunity ruling can be reviewed until the end.
explanation.memoryAid: Immunity from suit can justify early review when the issue is legal.

authoring.contentWarning: Original MBE-style practice draft. Not an official, released, licensed, actual, or NCBE question. A qualified attorney should verify current law, wording, taxonomy, and distractor validity before publication or external use.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: UCC course of usage and trade usage explaining an integrated writing
questions.stem: A cabinetmaker contracted in writing to buy 5,000 board feet of “No. 2 pine” from a lumber merchant. Both parties were merchants in the local woodworking market. Their written contract stated the price, delivery date, quantity, and grade, and it also stated that the writing was the parties' complete agreement. The writing did not mention moisture content. In the local trade, however, “No. 2 pine” sold for interior cabinetry ordinarily means kiln-dried pine unless the contract states otherwise. The seller delivered green pine that met the general grading rules for No. 2 pine but could not be used for interior cabinetry without expensive drying. The buyer offers evidence of the trade usage. The buyer had ordered the lumber for a line of built-in cabinets, a use that the seller knew from the parties' prior transactions. The seller had supplied kiln-dried pine under the same grade description twice before.
questions.call: Is the trade-usage evidence admissible to interpret the contract?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a complete integration clause excludes every form of trade-usage evidence.
choices.rationale: Under the UCC, trade usage may explain or supplement even a final writing unless it is carefully negated or cannot reasonably be reconciled with the writing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the usage explains the meaning of the grade term without necessarily contradicting the writing.
choices.rationale: Trade usage can give commercial meaning to contract terms, especially between merchants in the same market.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because trade usage is admissible only if both parties expressly mention the usage in the contract.
choices.rationale: The point of trade-usage evidence is that commercial background can inform meaning even without express recital.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only to prove that the seller acted negligently rather than to define the goods promised.
choices.rationale: The evidence is offered to interpret the contract term, not merely to show fault.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether trade usage may explain a term in a final written contract for the sale of goods.
explanation.governingRule: Under UCC Article 2, course of performance, course of dealing, and usage of trade may explain or supplement contract terms unless they are negated or cannot reasonably be reconciled with the express terms.
explanation.application: The term “No. 2 pine” can reasonably carry a trade meaning about moisture content for interior cabinetry. The usage does not necessarily contradict the grade term.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating the parol evidence rule as excluding all commercial context in goods contracts.
explanation.memoryAid: UCC writings live in their commercial setting.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Specific performance - land-sale contract
questions.stem: A seller contracted in writing to sell a small waterfront lot to a buyer for $420,000. The lot was one of the few buildable parcels on a lake where new subdivisions were prohibited. Before closing, a developer offered the seller $500,000 for the same lot. The seller refused to close with the buyer and offered to pay the buyer $80,000, the difference between the contract price and the developer's offer. The buyer had planned to build a weekend home for personal use and promptly sued for specific performance. The seller argues that money damages are adequate because the lot has a readily provable market value. The buyer had arranged financing, ordered architectural drawings, and was ready to close on the scheduled date. No zoning, title, or financing problem prevented the seller from conveying the lot as promised.
questions.call: Is the buyer likely entitled to specific performance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because specific performance is unavailable whenever market value can be estimated.
choices.rationale: Land is traditionally treated as unique, and specific performance may be available even when damages can be approximated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the buyer plans personal use rather than commercial development.
choices.rationale: Personal use does not defeat the uniqueness of land or the equitable remedy.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because land is considered unique and damages may not adequately substitute for the promised parcel.
choices.rationale: A buyer of real property ordinarily may obtain specific performance when the seller refuses to convey.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the buyer first proves that no other lot exists anywhere in the state.
choices.rationale: The buyer need not prove absolute impossibility of purchasing any other property.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a buyer may obtain specific performance of a land-sale contract.
explanation.governingRule: Specific performance is commonly available to a buyer of land because each parcel is treated as unique and legal damages may be inadequate.
explanation.application: The seller promised a particular waterfront lot, and the buyer promptly seeks that parcel rather than speculative monetary substitution.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that an appraiser's ability to assign a dollar value makes damages adequate.
explanation.memoryAid: Land sale: buyer can usually ask for the land, not just the spread.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: UCC buyer's cover damages and incidental expenses
questions.stem: A restaurant chain contracted to buy 500 commercial mixers from a manufacturer for $40 per unit, delivery by May 1. Two weeks before delivery, the manufacturer repudiated because it had found a more profitable buyer. The chain promptly bought 500 comparable mixers from another supplier for $46 per unit in a reasonable substitute transaction. To keep the restaurants supplied, the chain paid $300 in reasonable expedited freight charges that would not have been incurred under the original contract. Because the substitute supplier performed its own quality inspection, the chain saved $200 in inspection costs that it would have paid under the original deal. The chain notified the manufacturer of the cover purchase within a commercially reasonable time. The manufacturer does not challenge the substitute mixers as excessive in price or inferior in quality.
questions.call: How much may the chain recover from the manufacturer under the UCC cover formula?

choices.label: A
choices.text: $3,000, because damages are limited to the difference between cover price and contract price.
choices.rationale: The price differential is included, but incidental damages are added and expenses saved are subtracted.

choices.label: B
choices.text: $3,300, because the chain may add freight charges without subtracting saved expenses.
choices.rationale: Expenses saved because of the breach must be deducted from the recovery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: $2,800, because the chain must subtract the freight charges from the cover differential.
choices.rationale: Reasonable expedited freight caused by the breach is an incidental damage added to the differential, not subtracted.

choices.label: D
choices.text: $3,100, because the chain may recover the $3,000 cover differential plus $300 freight, less $200 saved.
choices.rationale: Cover damages equal cover price minus contract price, plus incidental or consequential damages, less expenses saved.

explanation.testedIssue: How to calculate a buyer's damages after reasonable cover.
explanation.governingRule: A buyer who reasonably covers after a seller's breach may recover the difference between cover price and contract price, plus incidental and consequential damages, minus expenses saved as a result of the breach.
explanation.application: The cover differential is 500 × $6 = $3,000. Add $300 in reasonable freight and subtract $200 saved inspection costs, for $3,100.
explanation.commonTrap: Stopping the arithmetic after the cover differential and ignoring additions or offsets.
explanation.memoryAid: Cover formula: cover minus contract, plus breach costs, minus saved costs.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Delegation of duties - original obligor remains liable absent novation
questions.stem: A homeowner contracted with a general contractor to renovate a kitchen for $90,000. The contract did not prohibit delegation, but it stated that the contractor was responsible for completing the renovation in a workmanlike manner. The contractor later hired a subcontractor to perform all cabinet installation and told the homeowner that the subcontractor would handle that portion of the job. The homeowner said, “That is fine as long as the cabinets are done by the deadline.” The subcontractor negligently installed the cabinets, causing $12,000 in damage. The contractor argues that the homeowner's approval released the contractor from liability for the subcontractor's work. The homeowner did not sign any new contract with the subcontractor and did not agree to look only to the subcontractor for performance. The renovation contract otherwise remained in effect.
questions.call: Is the contractor liable to the homeowner for the cabinet damage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because delegation of performance did not discharge the contractor's duty absent a novation.
choices.rationale: An obligor who delegates performance remains liable unless the obligee agrees to substitute the delegatee and release the original obligor.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only because the subcontractor acted intentionally rather than negligently.
choices.rationale: The contractor's continuing liability does not turn on the subcontractor's mental state.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the homeowner consented to the subcontractor's performance.
choices.rationale: Consent to delegation is not the same as a novation releasing the original contractor.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because duties involving construction can never be delegated.
choices.rationale: Many construction duties can be delegated, but delegation does not automatically discharge the delegating contractor.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an obligor remains liable after delegating contractual duties.
explanation.governingRule: A contractual duty may often be delegated, but the delegating party remains liable for performance unless the obligee agrees to a novation releasing the original obligor.
explanation.application: The homeowner allowed the subcontractor to work but did not agree to release the contractor or substitute the subcontractor as sole obligor.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating permission to use a delegate as a release of the original promisor.
explanation.memoryAid: Delegation changes who performs; novation changes who is liable.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Intended beneficiary - vesting by assent or reliance before modification
questions.stem: A nonprofit scholarship foundation contracted with a test-preparation company to provide 60 hours of tutoring to a named student preparing for a licensing examination. The contract identified the student by name, stated that the tutoring was “for the student's benefit,” and required the company to begin sessions by June 1. After receiving a copy of the contract from the foundation, the student emailed both contracting parties, “I accept this tutoring benefit and will rely on it for my exam plan,” and canceled another tutoring arrangement. A week later, before any sessions occurred, the foundation and the company agreed between themselves to cancel the tutoring provision because of budget concerns. The original contract did not reserve an unlimited right to modify or discharge the tutoring benefit after the student accepted it. The student had no realistic way to replace the canceled sessions at the same price.
questions.call: May the student enforce the tutoring provision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a third-party beneficiary can never enforce a contract unless the beneficiary gave consideration.
choices.rationale: An intended beneficiary need not furnish consideration to enforce a vested right.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the student was an intended beneficiary whose rights vested before the attempted cancellation.
choices.rationale: The contract named the student and the student assented and relied before the parties attempted to modify or rescind the benefit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the foundation and the company retained unlimited power to modify the contract until tutoring actually began.
choices.rationale: The original parties' power to modify can be cut off when the beneficiary's rights vest.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the test-preparation company also received a separate promise directly from the student.
choices.rationale: Direct privity between the beneficiary and promisor is not required for an intended beneficiary claim.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an intended beneficiary's rights vested before the original parties attempted to cancel the benefit.
explanation.governingRule: Unless the contract provides otherwise, an intended beneficiary's rights may vest when the beneficiary materially relies, assents at a party's request, or sues to enforce the promise.
explanation.application: The student was expressly identified and canceled another arrangement after accepting the benefit. That reliance and assent occurred before the cancellation.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming that the original parties can always revoke a third-party benefit until performance starts.
explanation.memoryAid: Beneficiary rights can lock in before performance if the beneficiary relies or assents.

questions.subject: Contracts
questions.subtopic: Satisfaction condition - personal taste and good faith
questions.stem: A collector hired an artist to paint a portrait of the collector's late parent for $25,000. The written agreement stated that payment would be due only if the portrait was “satisfactory to the collector in personal likeness and emotional tone.” The artist completed a technically skilled portrait using the photographs and descriptions provided. Several other artists testified that the work was competently executed, but the collector honestly felt that the expression in the portrait made the parent look stern and unlike the collector's memory. The collector rejected the portrait in good faith and refused to pay. The artist sued for the contract price. The artist did not allege that the collector invented the dissatisfaction to avoid paying or to obtain a lower price. The collector returned the portrait promptly and gave the same reason for rejection each time.
questions.call: Is the artist likely to recover the contract price?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because a reasonable artist would be satisfied with the technical quality of the portrait.
choices.rationale: For matters of personal taste or aesthetics, an honest subjective dissatisfaction standard generally applies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because satisfaction clauses are unenforceable whenever they make payment depend on one party's judgment.
choices.rationale: Satisfaction clauses are enforceable, constrained by good faith.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the contract made personal satisfaction a condition and the collector rejected the portrait in good faith.
choices.rationale: When satisfaction concerns personal taste, the promisor's honest dissatisfaction can prevent the condition from occurring.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because the artist used photographs rather than having the parent sit for the portrait.
choices.rationale: The method of reference is not the dispositive fact; the satisfaction condition and good faith rejection are.

explanation.testedIssue: How a personal-satisfaction condition operates in a contract involving taste or aesthetics.
explanation.governingRule: A satisfaction condition involving personal taste, aesthetics, or judgment is generally measured by the promisor's honest good-faith satisfaction, not by an objective reasonableness standard.
explanation.application: The portrait's emotional tone was expressly committed to the collector's personal satisfaction. The collector honestly rejected it, so the payment condition did not occur.
explanation.commonTrap: Using the objective reasonable-person test for a matter of personal taste.
explanation.memoryAid: Personal taste clauses ask honest satisfaction; commercial utility usually asks reasonable satisfaction.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Mistake of fact - specific intent to steal
questions.stem: At a crowded courthouse security checkpoint, a lawyer placed a black leather briefcase on the conveyor belt. Another lawyer, who owned an almost identical briefcase, accidentally picked up the first lawyer's briefcase after it came through the scanner. The second lawyer did not look inside until he reached his office, where he discovered expensive headphones and documents that were not his. At that point, embarrassed and hoping no one would notice, he decided to keep the headphones and throw away the documents. The jurisdiction defines theft offenses according to common-law larceny principles. The checkpoint was moving quickly, and several lawyers had placed similar bags on the belt at the same time. The second lawyer had not noticed any identifying tag before leaving the courthouse.
questions.call: Is the second lawyer guilty of larceny of the briefcase when he picked it up at the checkpoint?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because he intentionally took property that belonged to another person.
choices.rationale: Larceny requires intent to permanently deprive at the time of the taking; an honest mistake can negate that intent.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because his later decision to keep the headphones relates back to the original taking.
choices.rationale: A later-formed intent does not automatically convert an innocent taking into larceny unless a continuing-trespass theory applies, which is not present when the original taking was not trespassory.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, but only because briefcases are not property that can be the subject of larceny.
choices.rationale: A briefcase is personal property capable of being stolen; the issue is intent at taking.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because he honestly believed the briefcase was his when he took it and lacked intent to steal at that moment.
choices.rationale: An honest mistake of fact negated the required larcenous intent when possession was taken.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an honest mistake of fact negates the specific intent required for larceny.
explanation.governingRule: Common-law larceny requires a trespassory taking and carrying away of another's personal property with intent to permanently deprive at the time of the taking. An honest mistake of fact can negate that specific intent.
explanation.application: The second lawyer believed the briefcase was his when he picked it up. His later misconduct may support other liability, but it does not make the initial taking larceny.
explanation.commonTrap: Judging the original taking by the defendant's later dishonest decision.
explanation.memoryAid: Larceny intent must exist when the taking occurs.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Felony murder - death caused during arson
questions.stem: A building owner was struggling financially and decided to burn down a vacant-looking warehouse to collect insurance proceeds. Late at night, after checking only the front office, the owner poured gasoline in a storage room and ignited it. Unknown to the owner, a security guard had fallen asleep in a rear break room after becoming ill during his shift. Smoke filled the building, and the guard died from smoke inhalation before firefighters arrived. The jurisdiction follows common-law felony-murder doctrine and treats arson as an inherently dangerous felony. The owner is charged with murder for the guard's death. The fire spread exactly as the owner intended it to spread through the storage room, but the owner expected firefighters would arrive before the entire structure burned. No independent act by a third party caused the smoke.
questions.call: Is the owner guilty of murder?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the death occurred during the commission of an inherently dangerous felony.
choices.rationale: Arson is a classic predicate felony, and a death proximately caused during the felony can support felony murder even without intent to kill.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only if the owner knew the security guard was inside the warehouse.
choices.rationale: Felony murder does not require knowledge of the victim's presence when the death is caused in the course of the predicate felony.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the owner intended only property damage and insurance fraud, not physical injury.
choices.rationale: Felony murder supplies malice from the dangerous felony, so intent to kill is not required.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a death by smoke inhalation is too indirect to be caused by arson.
choices.rationale: Smoke inhalation is a foreseeable and direct way in which arson can cause death.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an unintended death caused by arson can constitute felony murder.
explanation.governingRule: At common law, a killing caused during the commission or attempted commission of an inherently dangerous felony such as arson may constitute murder without separate proof of intent to kill.
explanation.application: The owner intentionally committed arson, and the guard died from smoke generated by that arson while the felony was underway.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming felony murder requires the defendant to foresee or know the particular victim.
explanation.memoryAid: Felony murder: dangerous felony plus caused death can supply malice.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Provocation and cooling time - murder versus voluntary manslaughter
questions.stem: A woman discovered text messages showing that her business partner had been secretly diverting clients for several months. The discovery enraged her. She went home, spent the night drinking and thinking about revenge, and the next afternoon drove to the partner's office carrying a loaded pistol. When the partner mocked her for “finally noticing,” she immediately shot him, intending to kill him. The jurisdiction follows common-law homicide rules. At trial, the woman argues that the killing should be reduced from murder to voluntary manslaughter because she acted in anger after a serious betrayal. The woman had time to call two friends, search online for the partner's office hours, and wait in her car until the partner arrived. She did not claim self-defense or defense of property.
questions.call: What is the most likely homicide offense?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Voluntary manslaughter, because any intense anger caused by betrayal is adequate provocation.
choices.rationale: Not every serious insult or betrayal is legally adequate provocation, and the long delay gave time to cool.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Murder, because the facts do not show legally adequate provocation and a reasonable cooling period had passed.
choices.rationale: The deliberate return with a loaded pistol after an overnight delay supports murder rather than heat-of-passion mitigation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Involuntary manslaughter, because alcohol prevented the woman from forming malice.
choices.rationale: Voluntary intoxication does not automatically negate malice, and she intentionally killed.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No homicide offense, because the partner's theft of clients legally justified deadly force.
choices.rationale: Property or business betrayal does not justify intentional killing.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether anger after a business betrayal and delayed retaliation reduces an intentional killing to voluntary manslaughter.
explanation.governingRule: Voluntary manslaughter requires legally adequate provocation, actual heat of passion, and no reasonable cooling time before the killing.
explanation.application: The woman waited overnight, armed herself, and returned the next day. The facts show planned retaliation rather than an immediate heat-of-passion killing after legally adequate provocation.
explanation.commonTrap: Equating strong emotion with legally adequate provocation.
explanation.memoryAid: Heat-of-passion mitigation needs adequate provocation and no cooling time.

questions.subject: Criminal Law and Procedure
questions.subtopic: Sixth Amendment right to counsel - deliberate elicitation after indictment
questions.stem: A defendant was indicted for armed robbery and appointed counsel. While the defendant was awaiting trial in jail, police placed a paid informant in the same housing unit and instructed the informant not to question the defendant directly, but to “keep him talking” about the robbery if the subject came up. The informant told the defendant that he had committed several robberies himself and asked whether the defendant's case was “as strong as the news made it sound.” The defendant then described his role in the indicted robbery. The defendant had not waived counsel, and the prosecution seeks to use the statement at trial. The informant was promised consideration on his own pending charges if he obtained useful information. The defendant's statement was not volunteered to a cellmate who happened to be present by chance.
questions.call: Should the statement about the indicted robbery be admitted?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because Miranda warnings are not required when a suspect does not know he is speaking with a government informant.
choices.rationale: That may address the Fifth Amendment Miranda issue, but the Sixth Amendment right to counsel had attached for the charged robbery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the informant did not conduct a formal police interrogation in an interview room.
choices.rationale: The Sixth Amendment deliberate-elicitation rule is not limited to formal stationhouse questioning.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the government deliberately elicited a statement about the charged offense after indictment without counsel or waiver.
choices.rationale: Once the right to counsel attached for the robbery, government agents could not deliberately elicit statements about that offense in counsel's absence.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because all statements made in jail are involuntary as a matter of law.
choices.rationale: Jailhouse statements are not automatically involuntary; the problem is deliberate elicitation after attachment of the Sixth Amendment right.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a jailhouse informant's elicitation of statements after indictment violates the Sixth Amendment right to counsel.
explanation.governingRule: After formal adversarial proceedings begin, the government may not deliberately elicit statements from the accused about the charged offense in the absence of counsel or a valid waiver.
explanation.application: The defendant had been indicted and appointed counsel. The informant's conduct was designed to prompt discussion of the robbery, the charged offense.
explanation.commonTrap: Analyzing the case only under Miranda and ignoring that the Sixth Amendment is offense-specific and had attached.
explanation.memoryAid: After indictment, no deliberate elicitation about the charged offense without counsel or waiver.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Excluding witnesses - party representative exception
questions.stem: In a federal civil products-liability trial, the plaintiff moved before opening statements to exclude witnesses from the courtroom so they could not hear one another's testimony. The court granted the sequestration order. The defendant manufacturer designated its risk-management director as the corporate representative who would sit at counsel table throughout trial. The director had investigated the accident and was also expected to testify about the company's recordkeeping and complaint procedures. The plaintiff then asked the court to exclude the director until after the director testified, arguing that the director's testimony might be influenced by other witnesses. The director had authority to assist counsel with factual questions during trial and to bind the manufacturer on logistical matters. The court had not found that the director was acting in bad faith.
questions.call: Should the court exclude the director from the courtroom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because any witness who may testify must be excluded upon request.
choices.rationale: Rule 615 contains exceptions, including for a party's designated representative.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because corporate parties have no right to a trial representative if the representative is also a witness.
choices.rationale: A non-natural party may designate an officer or employee as its representative even if that person will testify.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, but only if the director's testimony will be limited to uncontested background facts.
choices.rationale: The party-representative exception is not limited to witnesses on uncontested matters.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the manufacturer may have a designated representative remain in the courtroom despite the sequestration order.
choices.rationale: A party that is not a natural person may designate a representative who is not excluded under Rule 615.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a corporate party's designated representative may remain in the courtroom despite a witness-exclusion order.
explanation.governingRule: On request, witnesses are generally excluded, but Rule 615 exempts a natural-person party, a designated representative of a non-natural party, certain essential persons, and persons authorized by statute.
explanation.application: The manufacturer is a corporate party and designated the risk-management director as its representative. The director may remain despite also being a witness.
explanation.commonTrap: Reading witness sequestration as absolute and overlooking the enumerated exceptions.
explanation.memoryAid: Sequestration has exceptions; a party representative may stay.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Leading questions - adverse party on direct examination
questions.stem: A pedestrian sued a delivery company in federal court after being struck by a company truck. During the plaintiff's case-in-chief, the plaintiff called the truck driver, who was still employed by the delivery company and was named as an individual defendant. The driver gave short, evasive answers about where he was looking immediately before the collision. Plaintiff's counsel began asking leading questions such as, “You were looking down at the dispatch tablet when you entered the crosswalk, correct?” Defense counsel objected that leading questions are never allowed on direct examination. The driver remained aligned with the delivery company, and his own liability would rise or fall largely with the company's liability. The judge had not declared him unavailable or incompetent.
questions.call: How should the court rule on the objection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Overrule it, because leading questions may be used when a party calls an adverse party or hostile witness.
choices.rationale: The driver is an adverse party, and leading questions are permitted in that setting.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Sustain it, because leading questions are categorically limited to cross-examination.
choices.rationale: Leading questions generally are disfavored on direct but are allowed in circumstances including adverse parties and hostile witnesses.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sustain it unless the driver first admits that he intends to lie.
choices.rationale: A witness need not confess dishonesty before leading questions may be used against an adverse party.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Overrule it only if the plaintiff shows that the driver has no memory of the collision.
choices.rationale: Memory loss can justify leading in some circumstances, but adverse-party status is enough here.

explanation.testedIssue: When leading questions may be used on direct examination.
explanation.governingRule: Leading questions generally should not be used on direct examination except as necessary to develop testimony, and they are ordinarily allowed when a party calls a hostile witness, an adverse party, or a witness identified with an adverse party.
explanation.application: The plaintiff called the defendant driver during the plaintiff's own case. The driver is adverse, so leading questions are permissible.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating the direct/cross distinction as absolute.
explanation.memoryAid: Direct usually open-ended; adverse witness can be led.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Original-writing rule - proving contents of public records
questions.stem: In a quiet-title action in federal court, a landowner needed to prove the exact language of a deed recorded 40 years earlier. The original deed had been filed in the county recorder's office and, under state law, could not be removed from the public records archive for trial. The landowner offered a certified copy of the recorded deed prepared by the recorder's office. The opposing party did not dispute the copy's certification or accuracy but objected that the original-writing rule required the landowner to produce the original deed itself because the deed's wording was central to the case. The landowner was not offering the copy merely to show that a deed existed; she needed the precise metes-and-bounds language. The certified copy bore the seal and signature of the county recorder.
questions.call: Should the court admit the certified copy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the original-writing rule always requires the physical original when a party proves the terms of a deed.
choices.rationale: The rules provide special methods for proving contents of public records, including certified copies.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the contents of a public record may be proved by a certified copy.
choices.rationale: A certified copy is an accepted way to prove the content of an official record that cannot conveniently be produced.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, but only if the original deed has been lost or destroyed.
choices.rationale: Loss of the original is not required when a certified copy of a public record is used under the public-records provision.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because deeds are excluded from the public-records provisions of the evidence rules.
choices.rationale: Recorded deeds may be public records whose contents can be proved by certified copy.

explanation.testedIssue: How the contents of a public record may be proved under the original-writing rules.
explanation.governingRule: The contents of an official record or a document recorded or filed in a public office may be proved by a copy certified as correct, or by other authorized evidence if a certified copy cannot be obtained.
explanation.application: The original deed is part of the public archive and cannot be removed. The certified copy accurately reflects the recorded deed.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming the best-evidence rule requires the most inconvenient original even when the rules authorize a certified copy.
explanation.memoryAid: Public record contents can travel to court by certified copy.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.subtopic: Summaries of voluminous records
questions.stem: A wholesaler sued a retailer for failing to pay hundreds of invoices generated over a three-year supply relationship. The wholesaler's accountant reviewed 1,800 invoices, credit memos, and payment records and prepared a chart summarizing the monthly amounts billed, paid, credited, and outstanding. The original records are admissible business records, but presenting each record individually would take several days and would likely confuse the jury. Two months before trial, the wholesaler made all underlying records available for inspection and copying. At trial, the wholesaler offers the accountant's summary chart to prove the amount due. The accountant can explain the method used to compile the chart and can identify the source records for any line item. The retailer had the opportunity to prepare its own competing summary.
questions.call: Is the summary chart admissible?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the original-writing rule requires every invoice and payment record to be read into evidence.
choices.rationale: The evidence rules allow summaries of voluminous admissible writings when the originals are made available.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because a summary is admissible only if the underlying records are too fragile to be brought to court.
choices.rationale: The rule turns on volume and convenience, not fragility.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because voluminous admissible records may be proved by a summary when the underlying records are made available.
choices.rationale: Rule 1006 permits summaries of voluminous materials that cannot be conveniently examined in court.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the parties stipulate to the accuracy of every underlying invoice.
choices.rationale: A stipulation is not required; disputes about accuracy may be explored through cross-examination and contrary evidence.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a party may prove voluminous records through a summary chart.
explanation.governingRule: A party may use a summary, chart, or calculation to prove the content of voluminous writings, recordings, or photographs that cannot be conveniently examined in court, provided the originals or duplicates are made available for examination or copying.
explanation.application: The 1,800 records are admissible but cumbersome, and the retailer had access to them before trial. The summary may be admitted.
explanation.commonTrap: Thinking the original-writing rule forbids summaries instead of recognizing that Rule 1006 is part of the original-writing framework.
explanation.memoryAid: Voluminous records can be summarized if the other side can inspect the source.

questions.subject: Evidence
questions.category: Hearsay and circumstances of its admissibility
questions.subtopic: Prior inconsistent statement under oath at a deposition
questions.stem: In a federal civil trial arising from a stairway collapse, a maintenance supervisor testified for the building owner that he had never seen cracks in the stair supports before the accident. The plaintiff then showed the supervisor a transcript of his deposition taken in the same case. At the deposition, while under oath and represented by counsel, the supervisor testified that he had seen “several visible cracks” in the supports two weeks before the collapse. The supervisor admits making the deposition statement but now says his deposition memory was mistaken. The plaintiff offers the deposition testimony to prove notice of the defect. The deposition was conducted after notice, the building owner's lawyer cross-examined the supervisor, and the transcript was certified by the court reporter. The supervisor remains on the stand at trial.
questions.call: How should the court treat the deposition testimony?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Exclude it entirely, because a party may not impeach its own witness in a civil case.
choices.rationale: A party may impeach a witness, and the statement also may qualify for substantive use.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Admit it only to impeach credibility, because all prior inconsistent statements are hearsay when offered for truth.
choices.rationale: Some prior inconsistent statements made under oath in formal proceedings are excluded from hearsay and may be used substantively.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Admit it only if the supervisor is unavailable at trial.
choices.rationale: Rule 801(d)(1)(A) requires the declarant to testify and be subject to cross-examination, not unavailability.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Admit it for both impeachment and substantive use, because it was an inconsistent statement made under oath at a deposition and the witness is subject to cross-examination.
choices.rationale: A qualifying prior inconsistent statement under Rule 801(d)(1)(A) is not hearsay.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether prior inconsistent deposition testimony may be used substantively.
explanation.governingRule: A declarant-witness's prior inconsistent statement is not hearsay if it was given under penalty of perjury at a trial, hearing, deposition, or other proceeding and the declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination.
explanation.application: The supervisor testified at trial and can be cross-examined. His deposition was under oath in the same case and directly contradicts his trial testimony.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming all prior inconsistent statements are limited to impeachment.
explanation.memoryAid: Under oath in a formal proceeding plus current cross equals substantive prior inconsistency.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Privacy - public disclosure of private facts
questions.stem: A nurse at a small clinic accessed a patient's electronic chart for work-related reasons and learned that the patient was receiving treatment for a serious infectious disease. The diagnosis was not publicly known, was unrelated to any public controversy, and the patient had taken steps to keep it private. After a personal dispute with the patient, the nurse posted the diagnosis on a neighborhood social-media group that had hundreds of members, adding the patient's name and address. The posting was true. The patient suffered humiliation and sued the nurse for invasion of privacy based on public disclosure of private facts. The clinic had not announced any outbreak, and the patient's condition did not affect the safety of the neighborhood group. The nurse's post remained online for two days before it was removed.
questions.call: Is the patient likely to prevail?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the nurse publicized a highly private, non-newsworthy fact in a manner that would be highly offensive to a reasonable person.
choices.rationale: Public disclosure of private facts can create liability even when the disclosed information is true.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only if the patient proves that the nurse's statement was false.
choices.rationale: Falsity is required for defamation or false light, not for public disclosure of private facts.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because truthful information can never give rise to an invasion-of-privacy claim.
choices.rationale: Truth is not a complete defense to the public-disclosure tort when the facts are private and not newsworthy.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because disclosure to a social-media group is not publicity unless a newspaper or broadcaster republishes it.
choices.rationale: Communication to a large online group can satisfy the publicity requirement.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether public posting of a patient's private medical diagnosis supports a public-disclosure privacy claim.
explanation.governingRule: Public disclosure of private facts requires publicity of private information that would be highly offensive to a reasonable person and is not of legitimate public concern.
explanation.application: The diagnosis was private, non-newsworthy, and disclosed to hundreds of neighbors with identifying details. Truth does not defeat this privacy claim.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing defamation's falsity requirement with the privacy tort involving true private facts.
explanation.memoryAid: Private fact plus broad offensive disclosure plus no news value can create liability.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Defamation - private figure, public concern, and damages
questions.stem: A local newspaper published an article about safety problems at privately operated day-care centers. The article falsely stated that the owner of one center had been cited for leaving toddlers unsupervised near a busy street. The owner was not a public official or public figure. Before publication, the reporter relied on a handwritten note from an intern and did not check the publicly available inspection database, which would have shown that the cited facility had a similar name but a different owner. The owner sued for defamation and sought compensation for proven lost enrollment and reputational harm. The newspaper argues that the owner must prove actual malice to recover any damages. The article named the owner personally and identified the center by address. The owner does not seek punitive damages or presumed damages on this motion, only compensation for documented loss.
questions.call: What standard must the owner generally satisfy to recover compensatory damages?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Actual malice, because the article involved a matter of public concern.
choices.rationale: Actual malice is required for public officials/figures and for presumed or punitive damages in some private-plaintiff public-concern cases, but negligence can support compensatory damages for actual injury.

choices.label: B
choices.text: At least negligence regarding the truth or falsity of the statement.
choices.rationale: A private figure suing over speech on a matter of public concern may recover actual damages upon showing fault at least amounting to negligence.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Strict liability, because the statement was false and harmed the owner's business.
choices.rationale: Constitutional limits require some showing of fault for defamation involving matters of public concern.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No standard can be met, because newspapers are absolutely privileged when reporting on child-safety issues.
choices.rationale: Newsworthiness does not create an absolute privilege to publish false defamatory statements.

explanation.testedIssue: What fault standard a private plaintiff must meet for compensatory damages in a defamation case involving a matter of public concern.
explanation.governingRule: For a private figure plaintiff involving speech on a matter of public concern, the First Amendment requires at least negligence as to falsity for recovery of actual damages; actual malice is required for presumed or punitive damages.
explanation.application: The owner is private and seeks compensation for proven actual injury. The reporter's failure to check accessible records can support negligence.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming any public-concern article automatically requires actual malice for all forms of damages.
explanation.memoryAid: Private plaintiff, public concern: negligence for actual damages; actual malice for presumed or punitive.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Negligent infliction of emotional distress - close-relative bystander
questions.stem: A driver negligently ran a red light outside an elementary school and struck a child in the crosswalk. The child's mother was standing on the opposite corner waiting to walk the child home. She saw the car enter the intersection, heard the impact, and reached the child within seconds. The mother was not herself in the path of the car and was never in physical danger, but she immediately suffered severe emotional distress that required medical treatment. The jurisdiction follows the modern close-relative bystander rule for negligent infliction of emotional distress. The child survived but suffered serious fractures. The mother had no prior emotional condition, and a physician testified that her distress was a medically significant response to witnessing the collision.
questions.call: May the mother recover for emotional distress?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because a plaintiff must always be within the zone of physical danger to recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress.
choices.rationale: The modern bystander rule can allow recovery outside the zone of danger for close relatives who contemporaneously perceive the injury.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because emotional distress is never compensable unless accompanied by a direct physical impact to the plaintiff.
choices.rationale: Modern rules often permit recovery without physical impact in defined circumstances.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because she was a close relative who contemporaneously perceived the negligently caused injury and suffered severe distress.
choices.rationale: Those facts satisfy the modern bystander theory in jurisdictions recognizing it.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only if the driver knew before the accident that the mother was watching.
choices.rationale: The defendant need not know the bystander specifically when the bystander rule's requirements are met.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a close relative who witnesses a negligently caused injury may recover for emotional distress.
explanation.governingRule: Under the modern close-relative bystander rule, a plaintiff may recover for severe emotional distress after contemporaneously perceiving a negligently caused serious injury to a close family member.
explanation.application: The mother directly saw and heard the child's injury as it occurred and suffered serious emotional consequences.
explanation.commonTrap: Applying only the older zone-of-danger or physical-impact rules despite the stated modern bystander approach.
explanation.memoryAid: Bystander NIED: close relative, present perception, serious distress.

authorityNotes: Restatement (Third) of Torts: Liability for Physical and Emotional Harm § 48

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Prescription products - learned intermediary doctrine
questions.stem: A pharmaceutical manufacturer sold a prescription medication used to treat migraines. The medication carried a known risk of dangerous interactions with a common blood-pressure drug. The manufacturer provided physicians with an FDA-approved warning that clearly described the interaction, the symptoms to watch for, and alternative treatments. A patient's neurologist received the warning but did not read it carefully and prescribed the migraine medication without asking whether the patient was taking the blood-pressure drug. The patient suffered the warned-of interaction and sued the manufacturer for failure to warn. The jurisdiction follows the learned-intermediary doctrine. The patient had obtained the medication only through the neurologist's prescription, and state law did not require direct-to-consumer warnings. The warning itself was not hidden or written in technical language.
questions.call: Is the manufacturer likely liable on a failure-to-warn theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because a manufacturer must personally warn every patient who may use a prescription medication.
choices.rationale: Under the learned-intermediary doctrine, adequate warnings to prescribing physicians generally satisfy the manufacturer's duty.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because the patient's injury proves that the warning was legally inadequate.
choices.rationale: An injury from a warned-of risk does not by itself prove the warning was inadequate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, but only because prescription medications are exempt from all products-liability claims.
choices.rationale: Prescription products are not categorically exempt; the issue is whether the warning duty was satisfied through the physician.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the manufacturer gave an adequate warning to the prescribing physician, who served as the learned intermediary.
choices.rationale: The physician's failure to use the adequate warning generally does not make the manufacturer liable.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether adequate warnings to a prescribing physician satisfy a prescription drug manufacturer's warning duty.
explanation.governingRule: Under the learned-intermediary doctrine, a prescription product manufacturer generally discharges its duty to warn by providing adequate warnings to the prescribing healthcare professional.
explanation.application: The manufacturer clearly warned the neurologist of the interaction. The doctor's failure to read or act on the warning is not a warning defect by the manufacturer.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming the manufacturer must bypass the physician and warn the patient directly in every prescription-drug case.
explanation.memoryAid: Prescription warnings usually run through the learned intermediary.

questions.subject: Torts
questions.subtopic: Conversion - serious interference with personal property
questions.stem: A cyclist left an expensive racing bicycle at a repair shop for a tune-up. The shop mistakenly tagged the bicycle with another customer's name. A week later, believing in good faith that the bicycle belonged to the other customer and that the repair bill had not been paid, the shop sold the bicycle to a used-equipment dealer and kept the proceeds. The dealer promptly resold the bicycle to an unknown buyer, and the cyclist could not recover it. The cyclist sued the repair shop for conversion. The shop argues that it made an honest clerical mistake and never intended to steal the bicycle. The cyclist had not authorized any sale, pledge, or transfer of the bicycle. The shop could have matched the serial number to the cyclist's intake receipt before selling it.
questions.call: Is the repair shop liable for conversion?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because selling the bicycle was a serious exercise of dominion inconsistent with the cyclist's ownership.
choices.rationale: Good faith mistake does not prevent conversion when the defendant seriously interferes with another's chattel.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only if the shop knew the bicycle belonged to the cyclist when it sold it.
choices.rationale: Conversion can occur even when the defendant mistakenly believes it has authority to dispose of the property.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because conversion requires an intent to steal or permanently deprive the owner.
choices.rationale: Conversion requires intent to exercise control over the chattel, not intent to commit theft.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the cyclist's only remedy is negligence for the mistaken tag.
choices.rationale: The sale and loss of the bicycle are serious enough to support conversion, not merely negligent handling.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an mistaken sale of another's chattel constitutes conversion.
explanation.governingRule: Conversion is intentional exercise of dominion or control over another's chattel that seriously interferes with the owner's right to control it. Good faith is generally not a defense.
explanation.application: The repair shop sold the bicycle and made it unrecoverable. That is a substantial interference with the cyclist's ownership.
explanation.commonTrap: Importing a criminal theft intent requirement into the tort of conversion.
explanation.memoryAid: Conversion intent is intent to control the thing, not intent to steal it.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.category: The relation of nation and states in a federal system
questions.subtopic: Dormant Commerce Clause - discriminatory fee
questions.stem: A state enacted a statute requiring any company that processes recyclable glass generated outside the state to pay a $200-per-ton surcharge before bringing the glass to an in-state recycling facility. Glass generated inside the state could be processed at the same facilities without the surcharge. The state justified the law by saying that in-state recycling businesses were struggling and that the surcharge would help preserve local jobs. An out-of-state recycler that regularly ships glass to the state's facilities challenged the surcharge under the dormant Commerce Clause. Congress has enacted no statute authorizing the surcharge. The statute did not impose a comparable charge on in-state glass that created the same handling and environmental costs. State officials described the measure in committee hearings as a way to keep local processors competitive.
questions.call: Is the surcharge likely constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because preserving local jobs is always a legitimate state interest sufficient to justify economic discrimination.
choices.rationale: Economic protectionism is not a valid justification for facial discrimination against interstate commerce.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the surcharge facially discriminates against interstate commerce for a protectionist purpose.
choices.rationale: Discriminatory burdens on out-of-state goods are usually invalid absent a compelling local interest and no reasonable nondiscriminatory alternatives.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the dormant Commerce Clause applies only when Congress has enacted conflicting legislation.
choices.rationale: The dormant Commerce Clause can restrict state discrimination even without conflicting federal legislation.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only if the recycler proves that every in-state facility refused to process its glass.
choices.rationale: The constitutional defect is discriminatory treatment, not a complete exclusion from the market.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a state may impose a surcharge only on out-of-state recyclable goods to protect local business.
explanation.governingRule: State laws that discriminate on their face against interstate commerce are generally invalid unless the state can show that the law serves an important local purpose that cannot be adequately served by reasonable nondiscriminatory alternatives.
explanation.application: The surcharge applies only to glass generated out of state and is justified by local economic protection. That is the core concern of the dormant Commerce Clause.
explanation.commonTrap: Balancing the law as if it were facially neutral when it expressly distinguishes in-state from out-of-state commerce.
explanation.memoryAid: Facial discrimination plus protectionism is almost always fatal.

authorityNotes: C & A Carbone, Inc. v. Town of Clarkstown

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: State action - private company performing a traditional municipal function
questions.stem: A corporation owned all the streets, sidewalks, utilities, police patrols, and residential lots in a remote mining town. For decades, the corporation had operated the town as the only practical place for employees and their families to live, shop, worship, and gather. The town's main sidewalk functioned like an ordinary public sidewalk, and the corporation invited residents and visitors to use it for daily travel. During an election season, the corporation prohibited a community group from distributing leaflets on the sidewalk criticizing the corporation's influence over local politics. The group sued, claiming a First Amendment violation. No county or municipal government operated within the town's boundaries, and residents depended on the corporation's sidewalks to reach the post office, school, and stores. The group sought only peaceful hand distribution of leaflets.
questions.call: Is the corporation's restriction subject to the First Amendment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No, because the corporation is privately owned and private property is never subject to constitutional limits.
choices.rationale: Private ownership does not always end the inquiry when the private actor performs a traditional public function.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because political leafleting receives protection only on government-owned sidewalks.
choices.rationale: The issue is whether this privately owned town is functioning as the equivalent of a municipality.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because operating an entire town with public streets and sidewalks is a traditional public function.
choices.rationale: A private actor that performs the full municipal function of governing a town may be treated as a state actor for these purposes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Yes, but only because the leaflets criticized the corporation rather than a government official.
choices.rationale: The state-action analysis does not turn on who is criticized; it turns on the corporation's public-function role.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a private corporation operating a company town is subject to First Amendment limits.
explanation.governingRule: Constitutional restraints generally apply to state action, but a private entity may be treated as a state actor when it performs a function traditionally and exclusively associated with government, such as operating a municipality.
explanation.application: The corporation operates the functional equivalent of a town, including sidewalks that serve as public thoroughfares. Its restriction is therefore subject to First Amendment scrutiny.
explanation.commonTrap: Stopping at private title without considering the public-function doctrine.
explanation.memoryAid: Private actor plus traditional exclusive public function can equal state action.
