questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Procedural due process - occupational license suspension
questions.stem: A state statute provided that licensed home inspectors could have their licenses suspended only for “substantial professional misconduct” after consideration of specified factors. A state licensing board received an anonymous complaint that an inspector had missed structural defects in several homes. Without giving the inspector notice of the specific allegations or an opportunity to respond, the board immediately suspended the inspector's license for six months. The board identified no emergency, public danger, or need for immediate action. The inspector denied the allegations and sued under the Due Process Clause. During the six-month suspension, the inspector could not lawfully perform paid inspections anywhere in the state. The board's rules allowed hearings, but only after the suspension period had already begun.
questions.call: Did the board provide constitutionally sufficient process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because occupational licenses are privileges and never property interests.
choices.rationale: A license that may be suspended only for cause can create a protected property interest.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because an anonymous complaint is enough process when the state regulates a profession.
choices.rationale: Receipt of a complaint does not substitute for notice and an opportunity to respond.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because due process always requires a full jury trial before any professional discipline.
choices.rationale: Due process usually requires notice and a meaningful opportunity to be heard, not necessarily a jury trial.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the inspector had a protected interest and ordinarily was entitled to notice and an opportunity to respond before a nonemergency suspension.
choices.rationale: For-cause protection and the lack of emergency make predeprivation process necessary.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a licensed professional is entitled to notice and an opportunity to respond before suspension.
explanation.governingRule: Government deprivation of a protected property interest ordinarily requires notice and a meaningful opportunity to be heard before the deprivation, unless emergency or other special circumstances justify postponement.
explanation.application: The statute limits suspension to substantial misconduct, creating an entitlement. The board suspended the license without notice or response opportunity and identified no emergency.
explanation.commonTrap: Using the label “license” to assume no property interest exists.
explanation.memoryAid: For-cause license suspension usually needs notice and a chance to respond.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.subtopic: Freedom of expression - content-neutral time, place, and manner restriction
questions.stem: A city adopted an ordinance prohibiting amplified sound above a specified decibel level in residential neighborhoods between 10 p.m. and 7 a.m. The ordinance applies to music, political speeches, religious services, commercial advertising, and all other amplified messages. It was enacted after repeated complaints that late-night amplified sound was keeping residents awake. A political organization that wanted to use loudspeakers for a midnight rally in a residential neighborhood challenged the ordinance on its face. The city allows unamplified speech at all hours and allows amplified rallies in nearby commercial districts late at night. The organization does not claim that officials applied the ordinance selectively or because of disagreement with its political message. Its challenge is to the ordinance's timing and volume limits on their face.
questions.call: Is the ordinance likely constitutional?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because it is a content-neutral time, place, and manner regulation serving a significant interest and leaving alternatives open.
choices.rationale: The rule regulates noise level and time without regard to message and preserves other channels of communication.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only because political speech receives less protection late at night.
choices.rationale: Political speech remains highly protected; the ordinance is valid because it is content neutral and appropriately tailored, not because protection decreases categorically.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because any rule that affects political rallies must satisfy strict scrutiny.
choices.rationale: Content-neutral time, place, and manner rules are not subject to strict scrutiny merely because they affect political speakers.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because a city can regulate amplified sound only after each resident files a separate nuisance action.
choices.rationale: Government may enact generally applicable noise regulations without requiring individual lawsuits.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a content-neutral late-night noise limit in residential neighborhoods is a valid time, place, and manner regulation.
explanation.governingRule: Content-neutral time, place, and manner restrictions in public forums are valid if narrowly tailored to serve a significant governmental interest and leave open ample alternative channels of communication.
explanation.application: The ordinance regulates decibel level and hours, not subject matter or viewpoint. Protecting residential quiet at night is significant, and alternatives remain available.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating every burden on political speech as content based.
explanation.memoryAid: Neutral noise rules can regulate when and how loudly, not what message.

questions.subject: Constitutional Law
questions.category: The relation of nation and states in a federal system
questions.subtopic: Anti-commandeering - required state enforcement of federal program
questions.stem: Congress enacted a statute creating a national permit system for operators of commercial drones. Rather than creating a federal enforcement staff, the statute required every state police department to conduct background investigations of permit applicants, inspect applicants' drones for compliance with federal standards, and submit written recommendations to a federal agency. A state objected that the program would require thousands of hours of state-employee work and refused to participate. The federal government sued to compel the state police department to perform the assigned tasks. The statute did not merely require states to preserve records or refrain from interfering with federal officers. It affirmatively assigned investigation and inspection tasks to state employees on a continuing basis.
questions.call: Is the federal statute constitutional as applied to the state police department?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because Congress may regulate commercial drones under the Commerce Clause.
choices.rationale: Even when Congress may regulate private activity directly, it generally may not commandeer state executive officials to administer the federal program.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because Congress may not require state executive officials to administer or enforce a federal regulatory program.
choices.rationale: The anti-commandeering doctrine bars federal commands requiring states or their officers to carry out federal regulation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the Supremacy Clause makes every federal instruction to a state agency binding.
choices.rationale: The Supremacy Clause gives valid federal law priority but does not authorize commandeering of state officials.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because drone regulation is reserved exclusively to the states.
choices.rationale: The defect is commandeering, not lack of federal authority over the underlying private activity.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether Congress may require state officers to administer a federal regulatory program.
explanation.governingRule: Congress may regulate private actors directly and may preempt contrary state law, but it generally may not command state legislatures or executive officials to enact, administer, or enforce a federal regulatory program.
explanation.application: The statute assigns federal permitting tasks to state police departments and seeks to compel their performance. That is unconstitutional commandeering.
explanation.commonTrap: Assuming federal power over the subject matter automatically permits Congress to conscript state officers.
explanation.memoryAid: Federal law may regulate drones; it may not draft state police as federal staff.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Recording acts - shelter rule
questions.stem: An owner conveyed Blackacre to a teacher for value by warranty deed. The teacher did not record. One month later, the owner conveyed Blackacre to an investor for value. At the time of the investor's conveyance, the investor had no actual, record, or inquiry notice of the teacher's deed, and the investor promptly recorded. The investor later sold Blackacre to a developer. Before buying, the developer learned about the teacher's earlier unrecorded deed, but the developer nevertheless paid full value and received a deed from the investor. The jurisdiction has a notice recording statute and recognizes the shelter rule. The teacher never took possession, and nothing on the land would have alerted the investor to the first deed. The developer's deed from the investor contained ordinary warranty language.
questions.call: Who has superior title as between the teacher and the developer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The teacher, because the developer had actual notice of the teacher's deed before buying.
choices.rationale: The shelter rule can protect a transferee from a bona fide purchaser even if the transferee has notice.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The teacher, because the first conveyance in time always prevails under a notice statute.
choices.rationale: A qualifying later bona fide purchaser can defeat a prior unrecorded conveyance under a notice statute.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The developer, because the developer takes shelter under the investor's status as a bona fide purchaser.
choices.rationale: A transferee from a protected bona fide purchaser generally receives the benefit of that protection.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The developer, but only if the developer recorded before the teacher recorded.
choices.rationale: Under a notice statute and shelter rule, the developer's priority does not depend on winning a race to record against the teacher.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a grantee with notice may take priority through the shelter rule.
explanation.governingRule: Under the shelter rule, a person who takes from a bona fide purchaser protected by the recording act generally receives the transferor's priority, even if the later transferee has notice of the prior interest.
explanation.application: The investor was a bona fide purchaser without notice and recorded. The developer takes through the investor and is sheltered by the investor's protected status.
explanation.commonTrap: Focusing only on the developer's notice instead of the investor's protected position.
explanation.memoryAid: A buyer from a BFP often stands in the BFP's shoes.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Landlord-tenant - constructive eviction
questions.stem: A tenant leased a ground-floor apartment for one year. Two months into the lease, a pipe in the building's common wall burst repeatedly, sending sewage into the apartment's bathroom and hallway. The tenant immediately notified the landlord each time. The landlord promised repairs but did nothing for three weeks, even though a plumber could have fixed the pipe within a day. The odor and contamination made normal use of the apartment unreasonable, and the tenant moved out four days after the third sewage backup. The landlord sued for rent for the remainder of the lease term. The tenant photographed the conditions and allowed the landlord access to inspect. The landlord did not claim that the tenant caused the backups or unreasonably prevented repairs.
questions.call: Is the tenant liable for the remaining rent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because constructive eviction requires the landlord to physically lock the tenant out.
choices.rationale: Constructive eviction can occur when the landlord's breach substantially interferes with use and enjoyment, even without physical exclusion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because a tenant must stay and sue for damages rather than vacate.
choices.rationale: Vacating within a reasonable time is part of establishing constructive eviction.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, but only because every plumbing problem automatically terminates a lease.
choices.rationale: The issue is substantial interference caused by the landlord's failure to repair after notice, not any minor plumbing problem.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because the landlord's failure to correct a substantial interference caused constructive eviction and the tenant vacated promptly.
choices.rationale: The facts satisfy constructive eviction and excuse future rent.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether severe unremedied conditions amount to constructive eviction.
explanation.governingRule: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord's wrongful conduct or failure to act substantially interferes with the tenant's use and enjoyment, and the tenant vacates within a reasonable time.
explanation.application: The recurring sewage backups made the apartment unreasonable to occupy; the landlord had notice and failed to make easy repairs; the tenant left promptly.
explanation.commonTrap: Thinking the tenant must be literally excluded or must remain in possession to preserve rights.
explanation.memoryAid: Substantial landlord-caused interference plus timely move-out equals constructive eviction.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Assumption of mortgage - personal liability and deficiency
questions.stem: A homeowner borrowed $300,000 from a bank and gave the bank a promissory note secured by a mortgage on the home. Five years later, the homeowner sold the home to a buyer. The deed stated that the buyer “assumes and agrees to pay” the mortgage debt, and the bank received notice of the sale but did not release the homeowner from the note. The buyer later defaulted. The bank properly foreclosed, but the foreclosure sale produced $240,000, leaving a $40,000 deficiency after permitted costs and credits. The bank sued the buyer personally for the deficiency. The buyer made payments for two years before defaulting, and the bank accepted those payments without objection. No anti-deficiency statute or nonrecourse clause applies.
questions.call: Is the buyer personally liable to the bank for the deficiency?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the buyer assumed the mortgage debt and became personally liable for it.
choices.rationale: A grantee who assumes a mortgage is personally liable, and the mortgagee can enforce that promise as an intended beneficiary.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, but only if the bank formally signed the deed to the buyer.
choices.rationale: The bank need not sign the deed to enforce the assumption promise as a beneficiary.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because only the original homeowner who signed the note can ever be personally liable.
choices.rationale: A purchaser who assumes the debt can also become personally liable.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, because foreclosure always eliminates any personal liability for a deficiency.
choices.rationale: Foreclosure applies the security; a deficiency action may remain if personal liability exists and is permitted.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether a purchaser who assumes a mortgage is personally liable for a deficiency.
explanation.governingRule: A grantee who assumes a mortgage debt becomes personally liable for the debt, while a grantee who merely takes subject to a mortgage does not. The mortgagee may enforce the assumption promise.
explanation.application: The deed says the buyer assumes and agrees to pay the mortgage debt. The bank may seek the remaining deficiency from the buyer after foreclosure.
explanation.commonTrap: Confusing taking subject to a mortgage with assuming the mortgage.
explanation.memoryAid: Assumption creates personal liability; subject-to creates only risk of losing the land.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Easement by necessity - termination when necessity ends
questions.stem: An owner divided a rural tract into two parcels and conveyed the rear parcel to a buyer. At the time of severance, the rear parcel had no access to a public road except by crossing the retained front parcel, and the deed was silent about access. For several years, the buyer used a gravel lane across the front parcel under an easement by necessity. Later, the county built a public road along the back boundary of the rear parcel, and the buyer could reach the parcel directly from that road with ordinary driveway work. The front parcel owner then blocked the old gravel lane. The driveway work from the new road would cost a modest amount compared with the parcel's value. The buyer prefers the old lane because it is shorter and more scenic.
questions.call: May the buyer continue using the lane across the front parcel?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because an easement by necessity becomes permanent once it arises.
choices.rationale: An easement by necessity generally lasts only as long as the necessity continues.

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, because the easement by necessity ended when reasonable access to a public road became available.
choices.rationale: Necessity easements terminate when the necessity that justified them ceases.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, because the buyer used the lane for several years and therefore automatically acquired title to it.
choices.rationale: Use under an easement by necessity is not adverse and does not automatically create title or prescription.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because the original deed expressly reserved the front owner's right to block the lane.
choices.rationale: The deed was silent; the result follows from the end of necessity.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether an easement by necessity continues after the necessity ends.
explanation.governingRule: An easement by necessity may arise when common ownership is severed and a parcel is left without reasonable access, but the easement generally terminates when the necessity ceases.
explanation.application: The new public road provides reasonable access to the rear parcel. The justification for crossing the front parcel has ended.
explanation.commonTrap: Treating necessity as creating a perpetual access right regardless of changed circumstances.
explanation.memoryAid: Necessity easement lasts only while necessity lasts.

questions.subject: Real Property
questions.subtopic: Deeds - delivery and retained control
questions.stem: A landowner signed a deed conveying a vacation cabin to her nephew. The landowner placed the deed in an envelope and handed it to her longtime friend, saying, “Keep this in your desk. If I die before I ask for it back, give it to my nephew. I may change my mind after the holidays.” The friend agreed. The landowner kept using the cabin, paid all taxes, and told the nephew nothing about the deed. The landowner died two months later without asking for the deed back. The friend then delivered the deed to the nephew. The landowner's heir challenged the conveyance. The landowner had previously used the same friend to hold important papers that she later retrieved. The friend understood that the landowner could demand return of the envelope at any time.
questions.call: Did the nephew receive title under the deed?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, because the friend physically delivered the deed to the nephew after the landowner died.
choices.rationale: Delivery must occur during the grantor's lifetime; postdeath physical transfer cannot cure lack of earlier delivery.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes, because signing a deed automatically transfers title even if the grantor keeps control over it.
choices.rationale: A deed requires delivery with intent to make it presently operative.

choices.label: C
choices.text: No, because the landowner retained the power to recall the deed and did not intend a present transfer.
choices.rationale: The conditional instruction and retained right to ask for the deed back show no effective lifetime delivery.

choices.label: D
choices.text: No, but only because a deed to a family member requires consideration.
choices.rationale: Consideration is not required for a valid deed gift; the problem is delivery.

explanation.testedIssue: Whether handing a deed to a third person is effective delivery when the grantor retains the right to recall it.
explanation.governingRule: Delivery of a deed requires the grantor's intent that the deed be presently operative. If the grantor deposits the deed with a third person but retains the right to reclaim it, delivery generally is ineffective.
explanation.application: The landowner told the friend she might ask for the deed back and did not intend a present transfer. The later delivery after death cannot create title.
explanation.commonTrap: Equating physical possession of a signed deed with legal delivery.
explanation.memoryAid: Deed delivery depends on present intent, not just who holds the paper.
